title: Directional & Resultant て-Form — てくる / ていく / てある

meta.focus: Use てくる for actions/changes directed toward the speaker or arriving in the present; ていく for actions/changes directed away or continuing into the future; てある for states created by intentional past action.

sections.title: てくる / ていく / てある — Direction and Result
sections.summary: Japanese adds directional and resultant meaning to verbs by attaching くる, いく, or ある to their て-form. てくる moves the action toward the speaker or the present; ていく moves it away or into the future; てある leaves a lasting intentional result.
sections.whyItMatters: These three patterns appear constantly in natural Japanese — in stories, daily conversation, and descriptions of change. They let you express not just what happened, but where the action is headed and what effect it left behind.
youWillLearn: てくる — expressing movement toward the speaker or a change arriving at the present
ていく — expressing movement away from the speaker or a change continuing into the future
てある — expressing a resultant state from an intentional transitive action
The が particle rule for てある (affected object takes が, not を)
The contrast between ている (neutral ongoing state) and てある (intentional resultant state)

sections.meaning: ~ and come / ~ toward the speaker / a change arriving at the present
sections.explanation: てくる (te-form + くる) has four uses: (1) going somewhere, doing something, and returning — the action moves toward the speaker; (2) a change that began in the past and has arrived at the present moment; (3) the onset or start of a change; (4) continuing to do something up to now.
notes: Formation: conjugate the verb to its て-form, then add くる. 持つ → 持って + くる → 持ってくる (take and bring back).
Past tense: てきた (てくる → past). 寒くなってきた = It has gotten cold (the change arrived at now).
てくる expresses movement toward HERE or toward NOW. The direction is always inward — toward the speaker's location or the present moment.
Use てくる for gradual changes that have built up and reached the present: だんだん 寒くなってきた (it has been gradually getting cold).
Contrast with ていく: てくる = toward / arriving; ていく = away / departing.

parts.gloss: came — brought it here toward the speaker

examples.breakdown: 持つ (godan: to hold/carry) → te-form 持って + きた (past of くる) = brought here toward the speaker.

parts.gloss: came — the change has arrived at now

examples.en: It has gotten cold. (A change has arrived at the present.)
examples.breakdown: 寒い → adverbial 寒く + なって (te-form of なる) + きた = the coldness has built up and arrived at now.

examples.en: It started raining. (The rain has arrived.)
examples.breakdown: ふる (godan: to fall/rain) → te-form ふって + きた = the rain arrived / started coming down.

sections.meaning: ~ and go / ~ away from the speaker / a change continuing into the future
sections.explanation: ていく (te-form + いく) has three uses: (1) taking something and going away — the action moves away from the speaker's location; (2) a change that starts now and continues into the future; (3) beginning an action here and moving away with it.
notes: Formation: te-form + いく. 持つ → 持って + いく → 持っていく (take it and go / carry it away).
ていく expresses movement away from HERE or toward the FUTURE. The direction is always outward — away from the speaker or away from now.
For gradual future change: だんだん 暑くなっていく = it will keep getting hotter (moving away from now into the future).
Contrast with てくる: てくる = action/change moves toward; ていく = action/change moves away.
In very casual speech, ていく can contract to てく: 持ってく (gonna take it along).

parts.gloss: go — taking it away from here

examples.en: I'll take the food (away with me).
examples.breakdown: 持つ (godan) → te-form 持って + いく = carry and go, taking the food away from the speaker's location.

parts.gloss: go — the change continues away into the future

examples.breakdown: 寒く + なって + いく = the coldness is going / will continue to grow away into the future.

parts.gloss: go — continuing the action into the future

examples.en: I'll keep learning little by little.
examples.breakdown: おぼえる (ichidan: to memorize/learn) → te-form おぼえて + いく = keep learning going forward.

sections.title: てくる vs ていく — Direction

items.label: てくる
points: Action or change moves TOWARD the speaker or the present
Someone goes somewhere, does something, and returns here
A change has been building and has now arrived at now

parts.gloss: came here — brought it toward speaker

example.en: My friend brought a cake here.

items.label: ていく
points: Action or change moves AWAY from the speaker or into the future
Someone takes something and goes away from here
A change starts now and continues away into the future
Plain form: ていく; past form: ていった

parts.gloss: go away — taking it away from here

example.en: I'll take a cake to my friend.

sections.tip: Think of it as direction relative to the speaker: てくる = inward (toward me / toward now); ていく = outward (away from me / away into the future).

sections.meaning: ~ has been done (and the result remains) / ~ is in a prepared state
sections.explanation: てある (te-form + ある) expresses a state that exists because someone intentionally performed a transitive action. Unlike ている (which describes a neutral ongoing state), てある implies human agency — someone did it on purpose and the effect is still there. The affected object takes が (not を).
notes: Formation: transitive verb te-form + ある. 入れる → 入れて + ある → 入れてある (has been put in / is prepared).
The affected object takes が: お茶が 入れてある (the tea が has been prepared). The を object marker shifts to が in てある.
てある always implies intentional action by an agent — someone purposely set up this state. It is never accidental.
Contrast with ている: まどが あいている (the window is open — neutral state) vs まどが あけてある (the window has been opened — someone did it intentionally).
Common contexts: preparation before an event, things left ready for someone, a state set up in advance.

parts.gloss: is in a prepared state — someone opened it intentionally

examples.en: The window has been opened (intentionally, and is still open).
examples.breakdown: あける (ichidan, transitive: to open something) → te-form あけて + ある = the window is in an opened state due to intentional action.

parts.gloss: is in a placed state — someone set it there

examples.en: Food has been placed on the table (and is still there).
examples.breakdown: おく (godan, transitive: to place/put) → te-form おいて + ある = the food is in a placed state due to intentional action.

sections.title: ている vs てある — Natural State vs Intentional State

items.label: ている
points: Describes an ongoing or resulting state — neutral
Uses intransitive verbs (no direct object, no agent implied)
No implication of purpose — it just is that way
Subject is the thing in the state (が is subject of the intransitive verb)

parts.gloss: is in an open state (neutral, no agent)

example.en: The window is open. (No agent implied.)

items.label: てある
points: Describes a state resulting from intentional human action
Uses transitive verbs — someone DID something to create this state
Always implies an agent acted on purpose
The affected object takes が (shifted from the original を)

parts.gloss: window (が — affected object, shifted from を)
is in an opened state — someone did it on purpose

example.en: The window has been opened (by someone, on purpose).

sections.tip: The key is verb type and intentionality: ている uses intransitive verbs and describes a neutral state; てある uses transitive verbs and marks that someone purposely created that state.

examples.context: Noticing seasonal change — てくる (change arriving at now)

parts.gloss: came — the change has arrived at the present

examples.note: 寒く + なって + きた = the state of coldness has been growing and has now arrived. てきた = past of てくる.

examples.context: Returning with something — てくる (movement back toward speaker)
examples.note: 持って + きた = carried and came back. The book moved toward the speaker's location.

examples.context: Predicting a continuing change — ていく (change going away into the future)

parts.gloss: go — continuing away into the future

examples.en: It's going to keep getting colder.
examples.note: なって + いく = the change is going (away from now, into the future). It will continue to grow.

examples.context: Taking something on a trip — ていく (movement away from here)

parts.gloss: go — taking it away from here

examples.note: 持って + いく = carry and go away. The food moves away from the speaker's location.

examples.context: Tea prepared in advance — てある (intentional resultant state)

parts.gloss: tea (が — affected object, shifted from を)
is in a prepared state — done intentionally

examples.en: The tea has been prepared (and is ready).
examples.note: が marks the affected object in てある. Someone prepared the tea on purpose; the result remains.

sections.title: Weather Changes and the Mountain Trip (Casual)
sections.context: りきぞ and けん chat casually about the cooling weather and りきぞ's upcoming trip to the mountains.

lines.jp: 寒く なってきたね。もう 秋だよ。
lines.en: It's gotten cold, hasn't it. Autumn has arrived already!

lines.jp: そうだね。寒く なっていくね。
lines.en: Yeah. It's just going to keep getting colder.

lines.jp: 来週、山に 旅行に 行くんだ。食べ物を 持って 行くよ。
lines.en: Next week I'm going on a trip to the mountains. I'll take food along.

lines.jp: いいな！お茶も 持って 行く？
lines.en: Nice! Are you taking tea along too?

lines.jp: うん。お茶は もう 入れて あるよ。
lines.en: Yeah. The tea has already been prepared and is ready.

sections.instructions: Choose the correct てくる / ていく / てある form to complete each sentence.

items.explanation: てくる (てきた = past) expresses a change that has arrived at the present: なってきた。
ていく expresses movement or action directed away from the speaker: 持っていく (carry and go)。

items.before: お茶は もう 入れ
items.translation: The tea has already been prepared.
items.explanation: てある expresses a state resulting from intentional action: 入れてある (was poured/prepared, and the result remains)。

sections.title: Practice: てくる / ていく / てある
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.explanation: てくる requires the て-form of the preceding verb: なって (て-form of なる) + きた。

items.q: お茶 ____ もう 入れてある。
items.explanation: The affected object in a てある sentence takes が, not を: お茶が もう 入れてある。

items.q: お茶が 入れてある。— What does this sentence express?
choices: The tea has been prepared (result of intentional action)
Someone is making tea right now
Tea was made and brought here
items.answer: The tea has been prepared (result of intentional action)
items.explanation: てある expresses a resultant state from intentional action — the tea was put in/prepared and the effect remains.

items.q: 食べ物を 持って 行く。— What does this mean?
choices: I will take food (along with me)
items.answer: I will take food (along with me)
items.explanation: 持っていく means to carry something away from the speaker's location: 持って (て-form of 持つ) + 行く (to go)。
